{{Infobox religious building|image=File:YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Kahoku|qid=Q1347734}}|coordinates={{Coord|38.426047|N|140.313058|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Ōjin|qid=Q317997}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11632782]] Wikidata name: Yachi-hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=original at [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 12 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=original at [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 12 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox shinto shrine
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity = [[Hachiman]]
|image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}}
The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>

It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>

== festivals ==
The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />

Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox shinto shrine
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity = [[Hachiman]]
|image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}}
The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>

It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>

== festivals ==
The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />

Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>

== festivals ==
The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />

Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox shinto shrine
| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
| deity = [[Hachiman]]
|image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}}
The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>

It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />

== History ==
The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>

== festivals ==
The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />

Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Kahoku|WD=Q1347734}}|coordinates={{Coord|38.426047|N|140.313058|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11632782]] Wikidata name: Yachi-hachimangū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:33, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=original at [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:56, 12 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yachi-hachimangū]][[ja:谷地八幡宮]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Kahoku|WD=Q1347734|1=Kahoku, Yamagata}}|coordinates={{Coord|38.426047|N|140.313058|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}}}
{{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Hachiman]] |image=YamagatakenNishimurayamagun-YachiHachimangu-01.jpg}} The Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine is a historic shrine located in 224 Yachi, Kahoku Town, Nishimurayama County, Yamagata Prefecture<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Japan Heritage Site Yamadera and Safflower Experience » Yachi Hachiman-gu Shrine |url=https://yamadera-benibana.jp/experience/en/benibana/%E8%B0%B7%E5%9C%B0%E5%85%AB%E5%B9%A1%E5%AE%AE/ |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=yamadera-benibana.jp}}</ref>. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref>. It is a [[Hachiman shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to the [[kami]] [[Hachiman]]<ref name="bunpu">{{cite web |last=Motegi |first=Sadazumi |title=Shamei Bunpu (Shrine Names and Distributions) |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=776 |accessdate=23 March 2010 |publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=Japanese}}</ref>
It was established in 1091 by the renowned samurai commander Minamoto No Yoshiie, also known as Hachimantaro. The shrine has a rich history of preserving the Hayashike Bugaku court dance and music, which is recognized as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property. <ref name=":0" />This tradition has been passed down through generations of the Hayashi family priests for over 1,100 years<ref name=":0" />.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Yamagata Pref. Festival & Event｜TOHOKU Cruise Promotion and Cooperation Committee |url=https://www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp/tohoku_cruise/en/020/020/060/ev_yamagata.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.pa.thr.mlit.go.jp}}</ref> the priests of the shrine come from that family.<ref name=":1" />
== History == The bugaku costumes used in the Hayashike Bugaku performances are distinctively designed with bright crimson cloth, dyed using the benizome method. The surrounding Rokku district was historically inhabited by six families whose members performed alongside the Hayashi family. Descendants of these families continue to be responsible for Hayashike Bugaku performances to this day.<ref name=":0" /> The traditional dance is done on September 14-15 every year.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Benibana Museum (The Safflower Museum) |url=https://www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp/database/benibana/bunken/book/bmuseum/bmuseum3.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=www.lib.yamagata-u.ac.jp}}</ref>
== festivals == The main festival of the shrine is Donga Matsuri, which is the largest festival in the region. It features a procession with a portable shrine extending up to 2 km in length. Additionally, the festival showcases Yachi Yakko performers and Hayashi Yatai floats with musicians playing festive tunes. An essential part of the Donga Matsuri is the Yayu no bugaku, performed around bonfires at night, providing participants with a solemn atmosphere and a reflection on the eternal flow of time.<ref name=":0" />
Throughout the year, the shrine hosts various other seasonal events. These include the New Year’s Day Festival and Hatsumode on January 1, the Kiuri Tenno-sai and Chigaya-nowa-kuguri festivals in summer, and the Shichi-Go-San festival in autumn. These events hold significant spiritual importance for the local community.<ref name=":0" />
== References == {{reflist}}
